author: Jacobs
title: The Death and Life of Great American Cities
description: Cities are problems in organized complexity. Diversity — mixed uses, short blocks, old buildings, density — generates sidewalk safety, economic vitality, and trust.

author: Logan & Molotch
full_name: John R. Logan & Harvey L. Molotch
title: Urban Fortunes
description: Cities are 'growth machines' driven by coalitions of place-based entrepreneurs seeking rents. Growth is a political project, not a natural market outcome.

author: Jackson
title: Crabgrass Frontier
description: American suburbanization was produced by deliberate government policies — FHA, HOLC — that subsidized white homeownership while disinvesting from cities and minorities.

author: Warner
title: Streetcar Suburbs
description: Boston's streetcar suburbs were built by an unplanned partnership of railways and middle-class homeowners, creating enduring patterns of class segregation.

author: Wiese
title: Places of Their Own
description: African Americans were active agents in suburbanization throughout the 20th century, with aspirations shaped by class position, southern heritage, and racial experience.

author: Needham
title: Power Lines
description: Phoenix's growth and Navajo impoverishment were mutually constitutive — the same electrical infrastructure connected coal extraction to suburban air conditioning.

author: Rome
title: The Bulldozer in the Countryside
description: Suburban sprawl's environmental destruction helped catalyze American environmentalism. The bulldozer reshaped landscapes and sparked a political movement.

author: Kruse & Sugrue (eds.)
full_name: Kevin M. Kruse & Thomas J. Sugrue
title: The New Suburban History
description: Suburbs were not homogeneous white enclaves but diverse contested spaces. Challenges the stereotype of suburbia as politically conservative and culturally uniform.

author: Suarez
title: The Old Neighborhood
description: What Americans lost in the great suburban migration — walkability, diversity, communal life, the social fabric of urban neighborhoods.

author: Willis
title: Form Follows Finance
description: Skyscrapers are vernacular architecture shaped by real estate formulas, not architectural genius. 'A machine that makes the land pay.'

author: Isenberg
title: Downtown America
description: Downtown was actively constructed and reconstructed by identifiable actors — merchants, planners, boosters — not simply abandoned by suburbanization.

author: Cronon
title: Nature's Metropolis
description: Chicago and its hinterland were a single metropolitan system. Market relations created an urban empire and transformed the landscapes sustaining it.

author: Crockett
title: People Before Highways
description: Boston's antihighway movement synthesized civil rights and planning ideologies into citizen-led 'city making' that challenged technocratic modernism.

author: Davis
title: City of Quartz
description: LA is brutally divided by class, race, and militarized space. Successive elite growth coalitions reshape the built environment to serve capital while criminalizing the poor.

author: Sugrue
title: The Origins of the Urban Crisis
description: The 'urban crisis' originated in the 1940s-50s: deindustrialization, racial discrimination, and white defensive localism — not 1960s liberalism.

author: Ansfield
title: Born in Flames
description: Landlord arson destroyed tens of thousands of housing units because insurance 'brownlining' made buildings worth more dead than alive. Suppressed only by tenant organizing.

author: Harris
title: Palo Alto
description: Silicon Valley is the product of 150 years of racial capitalism in California. The 'Palo Alto System' — test early, invest aggressively — became a template for everything.

author: O'Mara
title: Cities of Knowledge
description: Cold War science funding reshaped American cities. Research parks were deliberate attempts to replicate Silicon Valley's military-academic-industry ecosystem.

author: Nicolaides
title: My Blue Heaven
description: Working-class conservatism grew from material conditions of suburban homeownership. South Gate's survival strategies transmuted into defensive racial politics.

author: Deverell
title: Whitewashed Adobe
description: LA matured through appropriating, sanitizing, and obliterating its Mexican past. Anglo elites built a 'City of the Future' by whitewashing its adobe origins.

author: Ley
title: The New Middle Class and the Remaking of the Central City
description: Gentrification driven by a 'new middle class' whose 1960s cultural politics led them to revalue inner-city neighborhoods — demand-side, not rent gap.

author: Osman
title: The Invention of Brownstone Brooklyn
description: Gentrification was grassroots cultural movement — postindustrial middle class searching for 'authenticity' against suburban conformity. They literally invented neighborhoods.

author: Zipp
title: Manhattan Projects
description: Urban renewal was a cultural project shaped by Cold War symbolism. Modernity-as-proof-of-democracy rhetoric was turned against renewal by its critics.

author: Avila
title: Popular Culture in the Age of White Flight
description: Film noir, Disneyland, Dodger Stadium, and the freeway did the ideological work of white flight — encoding racial hierarchy into the design of mass culture.

author: Colton
title: Big Daddy
description: Gardiner's personality made Metropolitan Toronto viable but locked it into expressways and suburban expansion. Costs became visible only after his departure.

author: Harris
title: Creeping Conformity
description: Canadian suburbs before WWII were remarkably diverse. Federal housing policy and corporate developers manufactured the conformity critics attacked in the 1950s.

author: Stelter & Artibise (eds.)
full_name: Gilbert A. Stelter & Alan F.J. Artibise
title: The Canadian City
description: Canadian cities were active agents in the country's development, not passive byproducts of frontier settlement. Challenges Canadian historiography's rural bias.

author: Wilson
title: The Culture of Nature
description: Nature is a cultural product shaped by tourism, advertising, and industry. Domination of nature is inseparable from domination in social relations.

author: Light
title: The Nature of Cities
description: Urban professionals deployed ecological metaphors to understand cities 1920-1960. Ecological visions shaped planning before modern environmentalism.

author: Bonnell & Kheraj
title: Urban Environmental History in Anglophone Canada (article)
description: Canadian urban environmental history has been neglected. The article maps omissions and opportunities for integrating environmental analysis into Canadian urban history.

author: Glass
title: Cracked Foundation
description: Debt and inequality in suburban America. The suburban dream rests on a cracked financial foundation that reproduces and deepens class stratification.
